A1.name: Network timeout cascade
A1.root_cause: Network link failure or host unreachable — KAFK transaction failures and ATMA DISCONNECT events indicate upstream connectivity loss.
steps: Check network switch and cable connections for the affected ATM
Verify host reachability via ping or traceroute from the ATM management network
Review KAFK consumer group lag — if elevated, restart the Kafka consumer service
Inspect TERM logs for connection timeout patterns and repeat failure sequences
A1.explanation: Triggered by KAFK transaction_failure_reason containing network keywords AND ATMA NETWORK_DISCONNECT events on the same ATM within the monitoring window.

A2.name: Cash cassette depletion
A2.root_cause: Cash cassette is empty or critically low — ATMH hardware sensor reports CRITICAL severity and KAFK records cash-related transaction failure reasons.
steps: Dispatch cash replenishment team to the ATM location immediately
Confirm cassette status via ATMH hardware sensor reading before dispatch
Update ATM service status in the management system to reflect out-of-service state
Verify resolution after replenishment by monitoring KAFK transaction success rate
A2.explanation: Triggered by ATMH event_type CASSETTE_EMPTY or CASSETTE_LOW at CRITICAL or WARNING severity, corroborated by KAFK cash-related failure reasons.

A4.name: Container restart loop
A4.root_cause: Application container is OOM-killed or crash-looping — GCP reports elevated restart_count and TERM shows fatal exception or OOM events.
steps: Restart the ATM application container via the service orchestrator
Check GCP container logs for OOM killer messages and memory limit breaches
Review TERM logs for exception class and stack trace to identify root failure
Increase container memory limit if OOM is confirmed and reschedule the pod
A4.explanation: Triggered by GCP restart_count exceeding threshold OR TERM log_level FATAL with OOM or container exception class in the monitoring window.

A5.name: Performance degradation
A5.root_cause: Transaction processing latency is elevated — KAFK reports high response_time_ms and reduced transaction_success_rate indicating throughput bottleneck.
steps: Review KAFK response_time_ms trend for the affected ATM over the last 6 hours
Check WINOS CPU and memory usage — sustained high resource pressure degrades transaction throughput
Restart the ATM application service if transaction success rate drops below 70%
Escalate to the hardware team if ATMH also reports concurrent sensor warnings
A5.explanation: Triggered by KAFK response_time_ms above the configured threshold with transaction_success_rate below the acceptable level in the same observation window.

A6.name: OS memory pressure
A6.root_cause: Windows host CPU or memory utilisation is critically elevated — WINOS reports sustained high values that constrain ATM application performance.
steps: Identify top CPU-consuming processes on the WINOS host using task manager or remote tools
Free system memory by restarting non-critical background services
Check WINOS network_errors count — elevated values indicate resource starvation affecting connectivity
Schedule a host restart during the next maintenance window if pressure persists
A6.explanation: Triggered by WINOS cpu_usage_percent exceeding 80% or memory_usage_percent exceeding 90% within the monitoring window.

A7.name: Out-of-order Kafka event
A7.root_cause: KAFK messages are arriving out of sequence or carry invalid offsets, indicating a stream integrity issue or producer misconfiguration.
steps: Check KAFK consumer group offset lag for the affected ATM topic partition
Verify producer message ordering guarantees in the ATM application configuration
Review kafka_offset sequence values in KAFK records for the affected ATM
Reset consumer group offset to a known-good checkpoint if ordering corruption is confirmed
A7.explanation: Triggered by KAFK kafka_offset sequence anomaly or out-of-order event arrival detected by the pipeline integrity check.
